bins.name: Cost Causation Over Blanket Rules
bins.desc: The filers strongly support cost-causation principles but oppose applying a blanket 100% upgrade cost mandate to all new loads. They argue that speed-driven, large-scale entrants like data centers should bear full 'but for' upgrade costs, while manufacturers with methodical growth plans should continue under the existing beneficiary-pays framework. Net billing for behind-the-meter generation must also be preserved.
quotes: Cost impacts cannot be measured solely in terms of the direct cost of infrastructure. Speed-to-market demands, volatility in load forecasts, and the potential for stranded assets must also be considered in evaluating cost responsibility.

mandating 100% cost responsibility for all network upgrades triggered by a new large load interconnection would bring an unjust and unreasonable result in some cases, particularly where growth is methodical, planned, and fits within the existing framework, and where existing customers in the impacted utility zone are generally allocated costs of new network upgrades that benefit them.
Requiring All New Loads, Including Manufacturers, to Be Responsible for 100% of Network Upgrades They Are Assigned, Is Contrary to Longstanding Cost Causation Principles.
Customers with behind-the-meter generation should be billed on a net basis (accounting for their net demand requirements) and not on a gross load basis.
the Commission should also evaluate the potential refinements as to how the cost causation and beneficiary pays principles are applied in the context of different large load interconnection requests.

bins.name: Differentiated Upgrade Cost Rules
bins.desc: The filers oppose the ANOPR's proposal to require all new large loads to pay 100% of assigned network upgrade costs. They argue this is unjust for manufacturers whose growth is planned and fits existing frameworks, and that the Commission must refine cost-causation methodology to distinguish speed-driven loads from methodical industrial customers.
quotes: mandating 100% cost responsibility for all network upgrades triggered by a new large load interconnection would bring an unjust and unreasonable result in some cases, particularly where growth is methodical, planned, and fits within the existing framework, and where existing customers in the impacted utility zone are generally allocated costs of new network upgrades that benefit them.
Requiring All New Loads, Including Manufacturers, to Be Responsible for 100% of Network Upgrades They Are Assigned, Is Contrary to Longstanding Cost Causation Principles.
the Commission should also evaluate the potential refinements as to how the cost causation and beneficiary pays principles are applied in the context of different large load interconnection requests.

bins.name: Preserve State Retail Jurisdiction
bins.desc: The filers acknowledge FERC has jurisdiction over transmission-level interconnections but insist it must not override state authority over retail ratemaking, new customer service applications, and distribution service. They support a FERC role limited to transmission system studies and optional fast-lane processes, not a mandatory federalized regime for all large loads.
quotes: mandating all new loads greater than 20 MW to go through a separate, new, federal process could cause significant negative impacts, including: disrupting existing processes; triggering confusion between state and federal organizations and regulators; creating business uncertainty; and diverting limited and scarce resources from staff at the Commission, at RTOs/ISOs, and at public utilities.

FERC must tread carefully to avoid intruding into state regulation over retail ratemaking, including new customer service applications, distribution service to end-use customers, and certain generation-related issues.

Regulations that affect processes currently managed at the state level could include a sunset provision, while regulations that are squarely focused on transmission grid transparency, load forecasting, and grid efficiency could be permanent reforms.

bins.name: Higher Size Threshold Needed
bins.desc: The filers argue the proposed 20 MW threshold is arbitrary and far too low, capturing manufacturers that are not driving today's grid challenges. They recommend a minimum threshold of 75 MW to 100 MW, applied only to new loads, and suggest using FERC's Seven Factor Test or NERC's bulk electric system definition rather than a bright-line MW figure.
quotes: mandating all new loads greater than 20 MW to go through a separate, new, federal process could cause significant negative impacts, including: disrupting existing processes; triggering confusion between state and federal organizations and regulators; creating business uncertainty; and diverting limited and scarce resources from staff at the Commission, at RTOs/ISOs, and at public utilities.

the Industrial Customer Organizations believe a 20 MW limit is far too low and would capture a significant number of manufacturing entities and other industrial and institutional organizations that do not have the speed-to-market and scale demands that are primarily driving today's grid challenges.
If a threshold is set by the Commission, it should be at a minimum of 75 MW-100 MW (or higher), restricted to new load added to the grid, and with reasonable and clear aggregation rules.

bins.name: Optional Fast-Lane Interconnection
bins.desc: The filers propose an optional federal fast-lane process called XL Connect for loads with urgent speed-to-power needs. Qualifying loads would pay full upgrade costs in exchange for a 90-day study timeline, face stricter credit and flexibility requirements, and still retain a distribution utility connection to satisfy state jurisdictional requirements.
quotes: the Industrial Customer Organizations respectfully propose that the Commission consider developing an elective, "fast-lane" interconnection approach that addresses regional transmission and resource adequacy impacts without cutting out the distribution utility or violating state-level tariffs.
XL Connect is an optional federal, fast-lane interconnection process for a large load connecting to the FERC-jurisdictional transmission system at transmission-level voltage.

bins.name: Flexibility Commitments in Fast Lane
bins.desc: The filers' XL Connect proposal requires large loads using the optional fast-lane process to demonstrate flexibility commitments, including curtailable load, behind-the-meter generation, or bring-your-own-generation arrangements, as a condition of eligibility.
quotes: the Industrial Customer Organizations respectfully propose that the Commission consider developing an elective, "fast-lane" interconnection approach that addresses regional transmission and resource adequacy impacts without cutting out the distribution utility or violating state-level tariffs.

bins.name: Transparent Load and Grid Studies
bins.desc: The filers urge FERC to require transmission owners and regional grid operators to conduct transparent load forecasting and system impact studies, including mandatory evaluation of grid-enhancing technologies and reconductoring before authorizing new transmission buildout.
quotes: the Commission could – to protect existing customers and promote an efficient electric system – mandate the relevant transmission owner or regional grid operator evaluate the use of advanced reconductoring and the use of GETs, and other efficiency and optimization measures for any new large load interconnection application and related studies.

bins.name: Elective Federal XL Connect Process
bins.desc: The filers' central structural proposal is an optional, elective federal interconnection pathway for speed-driven large loads, paired with continued availability of the state-level new customer service application process for manufacturers. This preserves jurisdictional lines while addressing grid stress from data center-scale demand.
quotes: the Industrial Customer Organizations respectfully propose that the Commission consider developing an elective, "fast-lane" interconnection approach that addresses regional transmission and resource adequacy impacts without cutting out the distribution utility or violating state-level tariffs.
XL Connect is an optional federal, fast-lane interconnection process for a large load connecting to the FERC-jurisdictional transmission system at transmission-level voltage.

bins.name: Transition Protections for Manufacturers
bins.desc: The filers ask FERC to establish a transition period with an effective date no earlier than 2027, and to exempt in-progress manufacturing projects with pending studies, transactions, or demonstrated site control from any new mandatory federal interconnection rule.
quotes: the new rule should not apply to new large loads where the customers have pending transactions, pending studies, pending new customer service applications, or demonstrated site control for a new or redeveloped project underway before the new rule is finalized.

bins.name: Sunset Rules Displacing State Processes
bins.desc: The filers argue that regulations displacing processes currently managed at the state level should include a sunset clause, while rules focused on transmission transparency and load forecasting can be made permanent.
quotes: Regulations that affect processes currently managed at the state level could include a sunset provision, while regulations that are squarely focused on transmission grid transparency, load forecasting, and grid efficiency could be permanent reforms.

bins.name: Net Billing for Behind-Meter Generation
bins.desc: The filers urge FERC to preserve net billing for customers with behind-the-meter generation, measuring transmission and capacity obligations based on actual net demand rather than gross load.
quotes: Customers with behind-the-meter generation should be billed on a net basis (accounting for their net demand requirements) and not on a gross load basis.

bins.name: Mandate GET Evaluation First
bins.desc: The filers recommend FERC require transmission owners to evaluate grid-enhancing technologies and advanced reconductoring before recommending new transmission upgrades for any large load interconnection, protecting existing customers from unnecessary infrastructure costs.
quotes: the Commission could – to protect existing customers and promote an efficient electric system – mandate the relevant transmission owner or regional grid operator evaluate the use of advanced reconductoring and the use of GETs, and other efficiency and optimization measures for any new large load interconnection application and related studies.
